Subtopic: Optimize data layout and query performance
Question:
As a data engineer, you are working on a task to retain only those versions of sales Delta table which are not older than last 7 days. You have observed that directory associated with this table contains a large number of data files for all the historical versions since inception.
What will be the query that you will run to delete the files of sales table older than 1 week?
CorrectAnswer: VACUUM sales RETAIN 168 HOURS
MemoryHook: 7 days = 168 hrs -> VACUUM ... RETAIN 168 HOURS

Question:
Consider that you have a view employees_updates which shows the data of employees who has either joined your organization or resigned from your organization in the last month. The view has a column called type which describes the type of an employee. There is a delta table employees which has data for all the employees working in the company.
You want to incorporate this Change Data Capture (CDC) feed by inserting data of new employees and deleting data of former employees from employees delta table. Which of the following queries will help you to accomplish this task?
CorrectAnswer:
MERGE INTO employees e
USING employees_updates eu
ON e.id = eu.id
WHEN MATCHED AND eu.type = “former“
THEN DELETE
WHEN NOT MATCHED AND eu.type = “new“
THEN INSERT *
MemoryHook: CDC: WHEN MATCHED->DELETE, WHEN NOT MATCHED->INSERT *

Question:

MemoryHook: Global temp views live at global_temp.view_name

Question:
Which of the below SQL DDL can be used to create a UDF to convert distance value from Miles to KM and vice-versa?
Note: If any other distance measure is provided then function should return the same value.
CorrectAnswer:
CREATE FUNCTION convert_distance(distance DOUBLE, measure STRING)
RETURNS DOUBLE
RETURN
CASE
WHEN measure = “Miles“ THEN (distance * 1.60934)
WHEN measure = “KM“ THEN (distance * 0.621371)
ELSE distance
END;
MemoryHook: UDF skeleton: CREATE FUNCTION -> RETURNS -> RETURN CASE WHEN

Subtopic: Use Auto Loader and debugging tools
Question:
As a data engineer, you are working on a task to load external CSV files into a Delta table using the COPY INTO command given below.
COPY INTO my_table
FROM ‘/path/to/files‘
FILEFORMAT = CSV
After running the command once, the schema of the incoming data has changed in one of the files due to addition of a new column and data load got failed. Which of the following SQL commands will help you to overcome this issue?
CorrectAnswer:
COPY INTO my_table
FROM ‘/path/to/files‘
FILEFORMAT = CSV
FORMAT_OPTIONS (‘mergeSchema‘ = ‘true‘)
COPY_OPTIONS (‘mergeSchema‘ = ‘true‘);
MemoryHook: COPY INTO: FILEFORMAT + FORMAT_OPTIONS + COPY_OPTIONS

Question: Assuming you have a Delta Lake table named customer_purchases that contains duplicate records based on customer_id. You want to deduplicate these records by keeping only the latest purchase record for each customer based on a purchase_date. How would you use the MERGE statement in Databricks SQL to achieve this?
CorrectAnswer: MERGE INTO customer_purchases AS target	USING (	SELECT customer_id, MAX(purchase_date) AS latest_purchase_date FROM customer_purchases GROUP BY customer_id ) AS source ON target.customer_id = source.customer_id AND target.purchase_date < source.latest_purchase_date	WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE;
MemoryHook: MERGE to delete old records: WHEN MATCHED AND condition THEN DELETE

Question:
Below sample input data contains two columns. One column is cartId (also known as session id) and the second column is called items. cartId | items

cartId | items

Question:
You are trying to calculate total sales made by all the employees by parsing a complex struct data type that stores employee and sales data, how would you approach this in SQL
Table definition,
batchId INT, performance ARRAY<STRUCT>, insertDate TIMESTAMP
Sample data of performance column
[
{ “employeeId“:1234
“sales“ : 10000},
{ “employeeId“:3232
“sales“ : 30000}
]
Calculate total sales made by all the employees?
Sample data with create table syntax for the data:
create or replace table sales as
select 1 as batchId ,
from_json(‘[{ “employeeId“:1234,“sales“ : 10000 },{ “employeeId“:3232,“sales“ : 30000 }]‘,
‘ARRAY<STRUCT>‘) as performance,
current_timestamp() as insertDate
union all
select 2 as batchId ,
from_json(‘[{ “employeeId“:1235,“sales“ : 10500 },{ “employeeId“:3233,“sales“ : 32000 }]‘,
‘ARRAY<STRUCT>‘) as performance,
current_timestamp() as insertDate
CorrectAnswer:
select aggregate(flatten(collect_list(performance.sales)), 0, (x, y) -> x + y)
as total_sales from sales
MemoryHook: Flatten nested arrays then aggregate: flatten -> collect_list -> aggregate

Question: A senior data engineer was running the following command on the orders Delta table. Question:
A data engineer is working on a task to process the data coming from an e-commerce application. He has got two tables called orders and customers whose schema along with the sample data is given below.

orders:
order_id bigint, order_customer_id bigint, items array<int>

order_id | order_customer_id | items
101      | 1                 | [17, 2, 59, 768, 46]
102      | 2                 | [34, 67, 82, 45]
...      | ...               | ...

customers:
customer_id bigint, customer_name string, customer_email string

customer_id | customer_name | customer_email
1           | Vicky         | vicky@abc.com
2           | Rahul         | rahul@xyz.com
...         | ...           | ...

He wants to fetch the details of customers along with number of items in each of the orders that they have placed. Which of the following code blocks can be used by the data engineer to complete this task?
CorrectAnswer:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name, c.customer_email, o.order_id,
COUNT(o.item_id) as total_items
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN
(SELECT order_id, order_customer_id, EXPLODE(items) as item_id FROM orders) o
ON c.customer_id = o.order_customer_id
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.customer_name, c.customer_email, o.order_id;
MemoryHook: Flatten array in subquery then JOIN: explode in inline SELECT

Question:
A data engineer is working on a task to transform the sales data of each quarter for different geographical regions. The schema and sample data of the sales table is given below.

col_name | data_type
year     | int
quarter  | int
region   | string
sales    | double

year | quarter | region | sales (in million USD)
2018 | 1       | east   | 100.67
2018 | 2       | east   | 20.34
2018 | 3       | east   | 40.06
2018 | 4       | east   | 40.52
2018 | 1       | west   | 105.98
2018 | 2       | west   | 25.32
2018 | 3       | west   | 45.76
2018 | 4       | west   | 45.21
2019 | 1       | east   | 120.35
2019 | 2       | east   | 110.51
2019 | 3       | east   | 80.49
2019 | 4       | east   | 60.21
2019 | 1       | west   | 125.42
2019 | 2       | west   | 115.8
2019 | 3       | west   | 85.13
2019 | 4       | west   | 65.77

He wants to create a view having different columns for each quarter of every year so that sales can be displayed in different columns for each quarter. Which of the following SQL commands can be used by him to complete this task?
CorrectAnswer:
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW sales_across_quarter
AS
SELECT year, region, q1, q2, q3, q4
FROM sales
PIVOT
(sum(sales) AS sales
FOR quarter
IN (1 AS q1, 2 AS q2, 3 AS q3, 4 AS q4)
);
MemoryHook: PIVOT in view: FOR quarter IN (1 AS q1, 2 AS q2, ...)

Question:
You have the following DataFrame representing store sales:
df = spark.createDataFrame([
(“Store1“, “Electronics“, 2000),
(“Store1“, “Clothing“, 1500),
(“Store2“, “Electronics“, 3000),
(“Store2“, “Clothing“, 2500),
(“Store3“, “Electronics“, 1000),
(“Store3“, “Clothing“, 1200)
], [“Store“, “Category“, “Sales“])
You want to group the data by Store and calculate the following:
Total sales for each store.
Maximum sales for any category in each store.
Count of rows for each store.
Which of the following code snippets will achieve this?

import pyspark.sql.functions as F
df.groupBy(“Store“).agg(
    F.sum(“Sales“).alias(“TotalSales“),
    F.max(“Sales“).alias(“MaxSales“),
    F.count(“Category“).alias(“RowCount“)
).show()
MemoryHook: Multiple aggs with F alias: F.sum(), F.max(), F.count()

Question:
A data engineer needs to compute DAU (daily active users) as the number of unique customer_id values per order_date. The orders_df DataFrame contains about 1 billion rows.
